narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream despite the challenges many face in this field, and we're here to help you understand the automated decision on your application in a clear way. Your profile includes solid school decile rankings of 7th in both Year 1 and Year 3—above average for most students—along with a middle-income family background, an undergraduate GPA of 2.60, an LSAT score of 28, and plans to attend full-time, which is the norm for over 90% of applicants. The admissions model, trained on past students' data to predict first-time bar exam success, estimated a 48% chance you'll face failure on that crucial test, tipping the scales just enough to reject your application as a precautionary measure for the program's outcomes. The biggest factor pulling toward this concerning prediction was your LSAT score, which is notably below the typical 36.7 average and had the strongest influence in signaling higher risk of bar struggles. On a brighter note, your Year 3 decile ranking stood out as a major positive force, pushing the model toward expecting success since it's stronger than most students' records. Similarly, your Year 1 decile helped in the same way, countering some of the risk with above-average performance there. Your undergraduate GPA and family income bracket played smaller roles but nudged slightly toward the failure prediction, as they're a bit below typical levels. Overall, while your academic deciles shine compared to peers, the LSAT's weight overshadowed them in this close call. This isn't a judgment on your potential—many with similar profiles succeed through targeted effort. Next, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to the average, strengthening your school deciles further if possible, and seeking mentorship programs tailored for Black women in law to build a standout reapplication. You're capable of turning this around; keep pushing forward.
